HOW TO LEARN, THINK AND
RESEARCH

2

In this module of the course you will learn how to research topics yourself and interpret
scientific research. There are already too many of these 'how to do research' articles and
even books, but most of them suck, because:
• They're overly abstract.
• They are longwinded and only cover the blatantly obvious.
• The politically correct approach to science they advocate is not actually practiced by
many good field researchers.
• They're excruciatingly boring.

So we'll keep this practical and concise without regard for what's politically correct.

5
How to study
You'll learn many new things in this course. To optimize your learning experience, here's
a guide with a scientific approach to study.

What is learning?
From a neuroscientific perspective, learning is the remodeling of the neuronal circuits in
your brain [2]. Psychologically, learning is integrating new information in your cognitive
map of the world. Your conscious awareness of learning is not all that you learn. Much
information is processed in your brain that does not enter your consciousness but may still
be stored in the brain: this is called latent learning.

The best study techniques
Extensive research has been on which study techniques are most effective. As it turns
out, the most commonly used techniques do not offer the most return on your learning
time. Rereading, summarizing and highlighting can be useful, but they don't enhance your
understanding as much as thinking more organically about the topics you learned about.
They focus too much on acquiring new information rather than remodeling your brain's
organization of this knowledge. See the following table for an overview of study
techniques and how they fare in scientific research.

6

Source

It's clear that the best way to enhance your understanding of a topic is deliberate practice.
To that end, we have added 2 sections at the end of every module that we encourage you
to make use of.
1. Learning objectives: you should go through this list and evaluate for each point if
you're capable of doing this.
2. Example exam questions: try to answer these. No answers are provided on
purpose so that you can't cheat and you're forced to formulate questions in the
Facebook group or live Q&A sessions to discuss the materials. Think back on when
you learned math in high school: you can see the answer to a question and think
'ok simple' yet when you then have to get to the answer yourself on your own, you
realize you haven't fully understood it yet.

7
At the end of the nutrition modules and at the end of the training modules we also have
case study weeks in the course. Here we'll provide you with real-life and theoretical case
studies on how to implement everything you learned thus far in practice. You'll find that
there are some intentional delays in the course materials before all the pieces fall together
to foster deliberate practice, repetition and reflection of the materials and thereby improve
your learning.

We also encourage you to create your own practice materials. The following techniques
are particularly effective based on the research.
• Creating flashcards with questions and terms to explain with the answers on the
back. If you prefer to use your smartphone, you can use the app Anki to create
digital flashcards.
• Print the course materials and black out key terms. Later, go through the document
and fill in the blanks.
• Implement the Cornell Note Taking System for each course module.

How to distribute your studying time
Many students wait until the last minute to study before their exams and then try to cram
in all the information. Psychologists call this 'massing'. As your teachers probably
preached to you on many occasions, massing is not an ideal learning experience. It
certainly beats not learning, but a plethora of research shows spacing your studying time
more evenly over time improves information retention, recall, and general task
headings: How to distribute your studying time

pages.text:
8
performance compared to massing [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]. This is called the 'spacing effect'.
Your memory thrives on repetition, especially for long-term retention. To make use of the
spacing effect, try to plan your learning schedule as follows if possible:
● Plan in one full evening or weekend day to study each week's course materials.
● Plan in 2 shorter sessions later in the week: one to reflect on the learning
objectives and another moment to answer the example exam questions. Based on
your performance, revisit the parts of the module you didn't fully understand yet.

Many students also fall for the planning fallacy: they systematically underestimate how
much time it will take to learn something.

As a result of the above, people learn better when they have set deadlines spaced evenly
apart. Self-imposed deadlines are also effective but not as effective as external deadlines,
presumably because they are not as strict. In line with this, we have spaced the course
contents at a very deliberate pace and we will close off the Comments section for each
module when we start discussing the next module. If you want to ask questions and
engage in the discussion, you'll thus have to keep up with the pace of the course. This set
course structure should also help reduce procrastination.

As a final tip, be strict with yourself. Free up a liberal amount of study time each week in
your agenda, such as Saturday morning, and stick to it. Making up excuses to rationalize
missed deadlines is one of the most common reasons people fail online courses.

10
Logical reasoning skills
Any intellectual endeavor relies on logical reasoning skills. In this section, we'll give you
many tips to improve your brain gains.

1. Beware of science mumbo-jumbo
'Big words' and technical jargon can give the impression of validity. Don't fall for theories
and plausibility: look for probability and empirical evidence of each theory. Broscience
often relies on theory without evidence.

So always ask yourself if you see any theory: what's the actual evidence supporting this?
Is there direct evidence from randomized controlled experiments on this?

11
2. Scientific skepticism vs. Bayesian thinking
Scientific consensus is a turtle moving along the sands of truth… Or whatever: it's a slow
process. Scientists only speak of something as a consensus or a well-established finding
if multiple research groups using different methods in different populations have replicated
the finding. That's why it took decades before the scientific community reached an
agreement that androgenic anabolic steroids enhance performance, that strength athletes
benefit from more protein than sedentary individuals and that multiple sets are superior to
single sets for strength development.

The issue here is that science is about knowledge and truth, not about the practical
application thereof. So the scientific perspective on the vast majority of the questions we
find relevant is 'Well, there's research to suggest X, but we face limitations A-Z, so we
need additional research before we can answer this question.' That's an answer that
doesn't benefit anyone and as a coach, it's a cop-out. If you have clients, you have to
advise them something. To get from facts to recommendations, you have to think like a
Bayesian, a rational style of thinking popular in economics that is inspired by the
statistician Thomas Bayes.

A Bayesian estimates a probability of truth for every belief and updates this probability
based on the available evidence. That probability is then multiplied by the expected pay-
offs to come to conclusions (economists call this expected utility theory).

In other words, Bayesian thinking prescribes how scientific evidence - statements of fact
- translate into advice and decisions. When you know someone's goals and the relevant
facts, Bayesian advice optimizes that person's decisions to reach those goals.

12

Example: You're a sponsored athlete and receive a new test booster. Other than a lot of
research establishing the supplement's safety, there are only 2 studies on its
effectiveness: 1 in favor and one against. A scientific perspective can only answer if it
works, not if you should take it. The answer to 'if it works' in scientific terms will be "No,
more research is needed before we can claim if it works." Yet a Bayesian may think as
follows. The probability that the supplement works is substantial, even though the benefit
is small. The cost of taking the supplement is virtually nil. So the benefit multiplied by the
probability that it works exceeds the cost of taking it. A Bayesian would probably take the
supplement; it's a virtually zero-cost, low-benefit decision.

13
3. There is no proof, only probability
Proof is the highest standard of evidence. It is normally reserved for logical statements,
such as mathematics. Empirical sciences - the sciences of observable facts - cannot
provide more evidence than probability by definition. Science does not prove supplement
X works; it says it is probable (95% probable, to be specific). When there is no empirical
evidence for a statement, as is often the case for broscientific statements, it can merely be
plausible.

The hierarchy in standards of proof can conveniently be remembered by the 3 Ps. Any
theory can sound plausible, but only with actual data can we say it's probable and we
have to accept that most things will never be proven.
headings: 3. There is no proof, only probability
theory can sound plausible, but only with actual data can we say it’s probable and we
have to accept that most things will never be proven.

pages.text:
14
4. It's only a single study!
Imagine there's a new supplement on the market called STX. You want to know if it's
worth buying, so you Google it. You find a forum where someone has tried it and says it
greatly increased his muscle growth. 5 others noticed the same thing.

A blogger did DXA scans to confirm the muscle growth he experienced and it was legit.
He then tested the supplement in a lab and the results match the label's ingredients.

After some more research, you find 3 more people who have confirmed their muscle
growth using MRI after taking STX.

Sounds pretty convincing, right?

The above evidence doesn't come anywhere remotely close to that of a single published
scientific experiment. No subject data, unknown motives and sponsorship, heterogeneous
measurement techniques, no control group, no randomization, no peer-review, no double
blinding, etc.

Don't underestimate the evidence provided by a study. Don't overestimate the worth of an
anecdote.

7. Objectivity vs. subjectivity
A statement is either objective or subjective. Objective statements are statements of fact;
they refer to a state of nature. Subjective statements are statements of feeling.

Determining whether a statement is objective or subjective is in itself an objective
question. "Ronnie Coleman is 9 feet tall" is actually a statement of fact, not an opinion. It is
a claim of the state of nature. A false one, obviously, but the statement is still objective.

More common examples are people trying to humbly add 'in my opinion' to a statement of
fact. Similarly, you'll often see people add "and that's a fact" or something along those
lines to strengthen their position. Doing so is at best redundant and often blatantly false.

And this isn't simply a semantic or moralistic distinction. It is crucial. Recall that Bayesian
thinking translates facts into decisions based on someone's goals. Any subjective
statement is entirely meaningless from a Bayesian or scientific perspective. This makes a
great difference in terms of how you give advice, because everything revolves around
someone's goals.

Many questions are subjective or worse, hypothetical (not referring to any facts), and thus
do not have a Bayesian answer. Example: "If you could only do one exercise, which would
it be?" You will be asked these questions, so you will have to help the person asking the
question to reformulate the question in objective terms. In this case, the person is
probably interested in optimizing his or her exercise selection given a time constraint. So
you will have to dig into the person's true intentions and not just take the question for
granted.

18

Without knowing someone's goals, which is often the case on social media and on
internet forums, the best a Bayesian can often do is provide a statement of fact. For
example, "supplementing BCAAs on top of sufficient dietary protein intake will not
increase strength development, muscle hypertrophy or fat loss." When you know that your
client's goal is one of the above and the client's dietary protein intake is already sufficient,
you can then advise them not to take BCAAs.

Here are some concrete, down-to-earth tips to give more Bayesian advice:
• Be mindful of the person's goals. It sounds so simple, but many coaches give advice
based on their own goals instead of those of their client, like powerlifters advising
clients interested in looking better to get fat to increase squat strength.
• Weigh the costs and benefits of the decision. Things aren't black and white.
• Use contingencies when the person's goals are not known to you. For instance, "If
money is not a concern, these supplements may be worth trying, as they provide a
small performance boost in some of the research."

19
8. Accept that your ego is weak
When you come across new evidence, you are strongly inclined to find an explanation that
fits with your prior beliefs. This robust finding in psychology is called confirmation bias.
The mind always tries to minimize cognitive dissonance between your ideas. This is
generally a good thing, because a Bayesian should not believe statements that contradict
each other. Having consistent beliefs is fundamental to being rational.

However, accepting that prior beliefs were wrong requires that your self-image takes a hit.
Another fundamental finding in psychology is that humans will go to great lengths to
maintain a positive self-image, since a positive self-image and happiness are closely
related. As such, the mind often finds it easier to 'explain away' new findings than to
accept that old beliefs were wrong.

What can we do? Realize that your mind is imperfect. You are psychologically biased
towards rejecting revolutionary information. Being a Bayesian requires fighting this urge
and overcoming your feelings with reason.
headings: 8. Accept that your ego is weak

Finding and filtering information
Our goal with this course is to provide you with all the information that's relevant for a
Personal Trainer or recreational strength trainee. However, we encourage you to do your
own research as well, especially after the course. In this section, we'll give you many tips
to turn you into Sherlock Science.

1. Always reading the full text is inefficient
PubMed contains over 27 million articles. About half a million more are published each
year. Even if you read 5,000 articles per year, which means reading 13-14 articles per
day, you're only covering 1% of the new literature.

'Fortunately,' exercise science and nutrition are very small scientific disciplines. It is
possible, if you're efficient, to consume the vast majority of the available information. Still,
hundreds of papers are published each month. The need for efficiency is particularly high
because the quality of these papers is very low compared to more popular scientific
disciplines.

Roughly a hundred interesting articles appear each month. 'Interesting' in this case means
the title suggests the article is relevant for a strength trainee. Then you should generally
read the abstract to determine if it's interesting and only if that piques your interest, should
you bother to read more.
headings: 1. Always reading the full text is inefficient

pages.text:
21
2. Filter your information
In this digital age, lack of information is rarely ever the problem. The problem is knowing
which information to trust. Person A says X, person B says Y. The following guideline
should help you cut through the noise very effectively: if someone doesn't normally
reference his or her work, you can generally ignore whatever facts they claim. Better yet,
don't read their stuff at all. You'll either learn nothing new or you'll gain a question, never
an answer.

Even worse, it's quite likely you'll remember a statement or argument without
remembering its source and forget that it's incorrect. Psychologists call this source
amnesia and it's a major reason for the unreliability of eyewitness testimony.

That said, you may obviously still benefit from someone's experience, perspective or
logical arguments. There just aren't many people who stick to those sorts of claims
without invoking pseudoscience or unsupported facts.

Filtering your information sources will greatly minimize the paralysis by analysis that's so
common in the fitness industry.

Here are several evidence-based fitness authors that Menno vouches for after having
personally met them and worked with them. Note that they don't always agree on
everything and their views may be biased by most of them being friends.
• Brad Schoenfeld
• Bret Contreras

Google Scholar

SciELO
• Excellent search functionality (Google)
• Moderately sized database
• Open access
• Great to find non-English language publications

There are a few other large academic search engines, but it's exceedingly rare to find an
article on any of them that you can't find with any of the above 3.

When searching, think of the keywords that pertain to the article you're looking for, not the
title of the paper. Academic search engines are based on a keyword system. You often
want to add 'resistance training' to your search to avoid getting tons of research on people
who don't even lift.

25
4. Master Google search operators
You should at least know the following:
• You can use quotes "" to search for keywords in that exact order. For example, if
you know the title of a study you're searching, you'll almost certainly get the right
study as the first hit if you search the study title surrounded by quotes.
For example:
"Sex Differences in Adaptations in Muscle Strength and Size Following Resistance
Training in Older Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis"
• You can use AND and OR to logically combine keywords and - to exclude
keywords. For example, if you're looking for a study specifically on strength training
women, you could add the following to your search:
women OR female -men -male "strength training" OR "resistance training" OR
bodybuilders OR "resistance-trained" OR powerlifters
• You can use before:[year] and after:[year] to limit your search to specific dates. For
example, if you're looking for a study that you know was published after the year
2018, you can add after:2018 to your search.
• When you're looking for the full text of a paper, you can use:
filetype:pdf "[journal article title]"
This limits the search to PDF files and searches for the exact title of the paper
you're looking for.

5. Don't limit yourself to the English language
Science is an English language enterprise; it's generally safe to say that all non-English
publications and researchers are not among the scientific elite. But occasionally, very
interesting research is published in another language, often Russian, Portuguese or
Spanish. In those instances, Google Translate is your friend; it can translate entire
documents for you.

Interpreting scientific research
Scientific research provides the highest quality of evidence on most topics, but it's not
always easy to interpret scientific publications. In this section, we'll teach you how to read
and interpret scientific publications.

How to read a paper
The most common paper structure in academia is the IMRaD model, illustrated below.

36

You'll often find a Conclusions section at the end of the Discussion with a summary of the
main findings and their implications.

Way at the end you often find the Acknowledgements section, which is what you should
check for the study funding sources to see if the authors have any conflicts of interest or
potential sponsorship biases.

While this is a useful structure to write up a paper, it is not the order you should read a
paper in if you want to critically evaluate a study. Many study findings make people go
'well, duh.' Yet when you ask people beforehand what they predict the study's outcome is,
they're often wrong. Here's how to read a paper you're genuinely interested in:
1. Read the methods section. This explains what they did.
2. Try to predict what the results will be.
3. Read the results section. This lists the results without any interpretation from the
authors.
4. If your prediction was inaccurate, think about why this was the case.
5. Read the introduction, which should discuss prior literature on the topic, and then
the conclusion, which has the interpretation of the study's findings from the authors.

If you read the introduction first, your view will always be influenced by the perspective of
the researchers. In spite of what you may have learned in Research Methodology 101, the
authors often write the introduction after having seen their own results, so it's a biased
account of the prior literature.

37

The different types of scientific publications
There are several types of publications. When we think of a 'study', we're often thinking of
a randomized controlled trial (RCT). These generally considered the gold-standard
method to obtain new information about a topic. By randomizing the participants to the
study groups and controlling for all variables, they provide more robust evidence than
most other forms of evidence. If a study did not randomize the participants to the study
groups, the design is sometimes called quasi-experimental.

The lowest level of evidence is a case study or report. This just describes what happened
without any control or intervention, often in a single individual. It's still better than an
anecdote, as it's validated and much information is provided.
headings: The different types of scientific publications

pages.text:
38
A case-control study is one step better, as it compares a group of individuals with a
certain common denominator, like a disease or outstanding academic achievements, with
a control group of individuals that don't have it.

Another level of evidence quality up we have cohort studies. Cohort studies are
essentially case-control studies over a long period. Ideally, cohort studies have a
prospective design, which means the methods were determined beforehand and the
researchers followed the groups of people in real-time. For example, a prospective cohort
study may compare students with low and high academic achievements and follow them
over their life to see who ends up happier.

These separate studies are compiled together in scientific reviews. Some of these are
'narrative' reviews, which means they have no pre-set structure. 'Systematic' reviews are
more controlled in that you have to specify the exact study inclusion criteria and show
which studies you found and excluded.

39
A systematic review can also be meta-analyzed, which is a method
to take the available information from these studies, notably the
effect sizes, and use statistical calculations to sort of 'average them
out'. For example, if 5 studies show a supplement works and 5 show
it doesn't, we can do a meta-analysis to serve as the arbiter to see if
the overall effect size is positive. It's important to note a meta-
analysis basically recycles information, so if you meta-analyze 5
crappy studies, you often still have poor quality of evidence.
'Garbage in, garbage out.' In this sense, it's better to think of
reviews and meta-analyses as separate lenses looking at the
literature, not as strictly higher-quality evidence. A single well-
designed RCT can provide stronger evidence than a meta-analysis
of 10 low-quality studies.

40

The hierarchy of evidence. Source

The above types of publications can have various structures. Here are several research
design structures you should be familiar with.

Cross-over design: This is a type of within-subject design where each participant
undergoes both treatments, ideally in randomized order with a wash-out period in
between. By having each person by his or her own control, you eliminate the variance
from having different people be in the different study groups. Example: Every subject tries
both a high-volume and a low-volume training program to see which works better.

External validity: The degree to which the results of the study can be generalized beyond
the study sample, to other situations, times and people. Do the same results occur in
other settings?

Internal validity: The degree to which the study's methods allow us to answer its
research question. In other words, whether we can establish causality between the
independent variable that was manipulated and the dependent variable that was
observed. If a study sets out to determine if carbohydrate intake influences performance,
but it does not control for energy and protein intake, it has low internal validity, as the
results are confounded by energy and protein intake. We cannot know which of those 3
factors influenced the results.

Interaction effects: An effect of a combination of two or more independent variables on a
dependent variable. For example, ephedrine and caffeine have an interaction effect: their
combined effect is greater than the combined effects of both in isolation. They work
synergistically, not just additively.
Outliers: An observation in the data set that is much different than the other observations in the data

Peer-review: The scrutiny of a paper before its
publication by other researchers. Ideally, this is done anonymously from both sides to
avoid bias, mediated by the editor of the publishing journal.

Statistics 101
Science relies on the collection of data. To interpret those data, we need to perform
statistical analyses. As such, if you don't understand statistical methods, you can't truly
understand any research findings. Unfortunately, most current scientific fields rely strongly
on frequentist statistics. These statistical methods are often very counterintuitive (unlike
say Bayesian statistics).

In the following lecture, we'll provide a simplified interpretation of common statistical terms
and analyses. Below we go into more detail and formal definitions.

➢ Lecture
Stats 101

Statistical significance
In exercise and nutritional sciences, you often want to know whether a certain treatment
worked better than placebo. Say training program A results in 2% growth and program B
results in 3% growth. Is A more effective than B? To determine this, we can test for
statistical significance with a probability (p) value. If the p-value is above 0.05, we say the
difference is not statistically significant. 0.05 is an arbitrary but firmly engrained
convention.

49
The p-value measures the probability of obtaining all results in the study under the
assumption that all the statistical model's assumptions are correct, including the null
hypothesis. The null hypothesis (H0) is the theory that there is no difference between the
study groups. As such, a low p-value means the chance of observing the results we see is
low if we assume there is in reality no difference between the groups. The results are too
extreme to be due to chance. As such, you can roughly think of the p-value as being the
probability that the results are due to chance. That actual probability is higher, 23% based
on some estimates, but the intuition is the same: a low p-value supports that there is in
fact a difference between groups.

Why can't we just say 3% is greater than 2% and leave it at that? There are virtually
always confounding factors that induce variance in any study. For example, some people
train harder than others. If you happen to have more hardcore people in one group than
the other, that may be the reason they grew more from a certain strength training protocol
than others, even though the protocols were equally effective physiologically speaking. If
you plot the muscle growth rates of both groups, you'll get 2 distributions. If there is no
overlap between these distributions at all, it's easy to see there is a difference in muscle
growth between these groups: the worst gains in one group were still better than the best
gains in the other group. However, in practice the graphs will overlap and a statistical
significance test then tells us if these 2 distributions are likely part of the same distribution
or if they're in fact different distributions.

50

Example: You plot the increase in total training tonnage of program 1 with sample 1 and
program 2 with sample 2. In this case, while the average increase in tonnage of program 2
is higher, this difference is so small relative to the variance in individual results that we
cannot say the distributions of the results are really different from each other. It's likely
sample 1 and sample 2 achieved the same distribution of results (plotted in orange).

In short, statistical significance means there is most likely a true difference between
groups, not just between-subject variance or measurement error. Assuming the study was
well-designed (high internal validity), we can conclude this effect was caused by the

51
independent variable of the study and, for example, program A was more effective than
program B.

Statistical significance vs. effect size
'Statistically significant' intuitively sounds like it means something is highly relevant, like
the effect is big. However, statistical significance does not directly measure the magnitude
of an effect. Large effects are more likely to be statistically significant, but very small
effects that are practically insignificant can also reach statistical significance. To look at
the strength of an effect, we can look at the raw values or the effect size, which is a
standardized measure of the strength of an effect. Effect sizes can be computed in
various ways, but most rely on dividing the average difference by the standard deviation.

The formulas for various effect sizes.

As an example to contrast effect size with statistical significance, in a very large study of
supplement X, we may find that testosterone increases by 3% in the experimental group
and this is significantly more than the placebo group. Testosterone production fluctuates

52
by about 50% across the day in healthy men, so 3% is a trivial increase and the effect size
is small.

As an opposite example, let's say a group of researchers try to replicate the above finding,
but their measurement instruments are older than Gandalf, so the measurements are all
over the place. They find a 178% increase in testosterone, but individual readings vary
from -340 to +421% and the finding does not reach statistical significance. In this
scenario, we cannot even talk about the effect size, since we cannot say with any
certainty that this finding is not just measurement error. However, depending on the
theoretical basis and the other literature, these kinds of findings can indicate a trend. So
just because statistical significance was not reached, it does not mean that this finding is
uninteresting. This brings us to the discussion of statistical power.

Statistical power
The power of a statistical test is the probability that the test will reject the null hypothesis
when the null hypothesis is false. It's identical to the probability of not making a type II
error (failing to reject a false null hypothesis): 1 minus power = type II error probability. For
those unfamiliar with the statistical jargon, here's a more intuitive explanation: statistical
power is the ability to detect an effect with a study when there is a truly an effect. It is the
sensitivity of a test: sensitivity in this case is both an intuitive and the proper statistical
terminology.

Here's an example in lay terms. You're conducting a study on the efficacy of supplement
X. There's the physical reality of its effectiveness, unknown to you, and there's the

53
outcome of your statistical test. Let's suppose that supplement X is the holy grail of
muscle building supps and transforms you into the incredible hulk in a manner of weeks. If
that's also what your test shows, everyone's happy. However, if supplement X actually
works, but your test says it doesn't, you've failed to detect an effect even though there
actually was one. That's a type II error and it's caused by a lack of statistical power. A
more sensitive test would have found an effect. (Lack of) statistical power is an important
reason why different studies can show different things.

What determines the statistical power of a test? A full power analysis takes into account
many things, including the design of the experiment, but there are 3 main factors.
1. The statistical significance criterion we call alpha. You can basically forget about
this, because the convention of using a significance level of 0.05 has become so
deeply ingrained in the scientific literature it may as well have been enforced by
law.

2. Sample size: The larger the sample size of a study, the easier it is to detect effects.
In our example, if the group receiving supplement X and the placebo group both
consisted of thousands of people, it's most likely that any difference between the
groups at the end of the study was caused by supplement X, because the large
sample size cancels out between-subject variance and ensures that the observed
values are very close to the actual population means (provided the sampling was
random and the study was well controlled). A sample size of only a few participants
has far less statistical power, because the effect of supplement X is obscured by
random variation or noise. In such a small sample, just one individual with, say, a
heart condition that makes him or her unsusceptible to the effects of supplement X

54
may be enough to fool the test into thinking supplement X doesn't work.

3. Third, there's the effect size, which is, as the name suggests, the magnitude of the
effect you're looking for. In our example, the effect size of supplement X is
extremely high, because it turns you into the incredible bulk. If that were literally the
case, you only really need a sample size of one or two people to know supplement
X is the bomb. Some things can have such a large effect size that personal
experience and anecdotes are enough evidence to see it's true. This was the case
with injectable testosterone when it first came on the market. Researchers debated
for decades whether injecting testosterone resulted in increased muscle growth,
mostly using absurdly small dosages, while it was blatantly obvious to bodybuilders
that had injected it that it increased their gains. In contrast, if supplement X were
only mildly effective, you'd need a far larger sample to detect its effects.

Type II errors can be contrasted with type I errors, which are false positives. A small
sample increases the chance of not just type II but also type I errors. If you happen to
have 3 genetic freaks in a study of 5 people, you may observe a statistically significant
difference between groups with a large effect size that has nothing to do with the study's
independent variable. It's just that those 3 people grew so much more muscle than the
others regardless of which group they were in because of their genetics.

55

Good studies perform an a priori power analysis, which means they estimate the power of
their study based on effect sizes from previous data or theory. An a priori power
estimation can be used to determine how many subjects are needed to reach 80% power,
which is commonly regarded as the minimum needed for a viable study.

Importantly, statistical power should not be calculated with the study's own observed
effect size after the study has completed (post-hoc). Using the study's own effect size
makes it useless, as it just becomes a recalculation of the p-value. If you question the
observed p-value, it's better to look at the confidence interval of the effect than post-hoc
power.

Confidence intervals
Scientific publications commonly report 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Confidence
intervals are interval estimates, as opposed to a fixed-point estimate. For example, if you
want to know how long the average dog is, you can measure 91 dogs and your average
may be 69 cm. That's probably not exactly the true average length in the whole population
of dogs. A confidence interval illustrates this by computing a range of values rather than

56
just reporting the average. The 95% CI is typically calculated as being 2 standard
deviations below the average up to 2 standard deviations above the average, as
illustrated below. In a normal distribution, we can say that 95% of our sample values fall
within this range. You can also compute the confidence interval for an effect, like that of
the difference in muscle growth rate between 2 groups.

Importantly, a confidence interval says 95% of the sample values fall within its range
under valid model assumptions. This does not mean 95% of the true (population) values
fall within this range. In theory, if one would repeat the study a near-infinite number of
times, 95% of confidence intervals should contain the true value (assuming all analysis
assumptions were correct in the first place), but a single confidence interval may not
contain the true value. As such, a 95% confidence interval does not mean there is a 95%
chance the true value falls within this range. There is such a statistic in Bayesian
statistics: it's called a credible interval.

57

However, we can still roughly interpret a 95% CI as a range of potential values that are
more compatible with the study's data than values outside of this range. It's particularly
useful to know if the CI spans the null. If the CI of an effect size, such as the increase in
muscle growth from a supplement, does not include zero, this is strong evidence for the
effectiveness of the supplement. If the effect size CI does include zero, we cannot exclude
the supplement doesn't work.
Noise vs. bias
Many people overlook this distinction. Plenty of study reviews try to assess the truth of a
study's findings by listing its strengths and weaknesses instead of, as a Bayesian,
inferring probability from statistical principles. You can't just list the limitations of a study
and then conclude 'therefore: screw this study.'

When you want to discredit a finding, you need to demonstrate bias, not just noise.

Example: A study finds that a greater meal frequency results in higher nitrogen balance in
trained but not untrained individuals. If the trained group had a higher protein intake than
the untrained group, that would be bias: the higher nitrogen balance in the trained group
could have been the result of this increased protein intake and not the meal frequency.

That's bias: a systematic flaw in the internal validity of the study that predisposes the
study's results in a specific direction.

58
Stating that nitrogen balance is an unreliable measurement technique is an example of
noise. Noise reduces statistical power. If you understood statistical power, you should now
understand that statistical significance in spite of this noise is actually an argument in
favor of the study's relevance. This suggests the effect size or the reliability of the effect
was high.

Most study limitations and weaknesses people use to discredit a study finding they don't
want to believe are noise, not bias. Common biases in exercise and nutritional literature
are:
• Not controlling for a treatment imbalance in calories, protein or physical work
between groups.
• Having a sample imbalance by having one group that is fatter or less experienced
than the other group.

Common sources of noise include:
• Small sample sizes.
• Large sample heterogeneity (variation) due to having people with varying training
experience in the experiment and the inherent genetic variability in responsiveness
to exercise.
• Measurement error: the best measurement instruments are expensive and exercise
science is a poorly funded field.

59
The above is all the statistical knowledge you'll need in practice as an evidence-based
personal trainer. However, if you're considering a career in academia or you're interested
in reading scientific studies yourself, here's a good article to further your education.

➢ Optional further reading
Statistical tests, P values, confidence intervals, and power: a guide to misinterpretations

If you want to learn even more statistics, Discovering Statistics by Andy Field is a good
book.

60
Learning objectives
For the exam, you should be able to do the following, though this module will not be tested
as extensively as the modules on training and nutrition.
• Effectively use search engines to find information.
• Find the full text of publicly accessible research papers based on partial
information, such as the authors, year and topic.
• Explain what statistical power, correlations, effect sizes, p-values and statistical
significance mean.
• Understand scientific methodology jargon.

In this module of the course you will learn how to research topics yourself and interpret
scientific research. There are already too many of these 'how to do research' articles and
even books, but most of them suck, because:
• They're overly abstract.
• They are longwinded and only cover the blatantly obvious.
• The politically correct approach to science they advocate is not actually practiced by
many good field researchers.
• They're excruciatingly boring.

So we'll keep this practical and concise without regard for what's politically correct.

How to study
You'll learn many new things in this course. To optimize your learning experience, here's
a guide with a scientific approach to study.

What is learning?
From a neuroscientific perspective, learning is the remodeling of the neuronal circuits in
your brain [2]. Psychologically, learning is integrating new information in your cognitive
map of the world. Your conscious awareness of learning is not all that you learn. Much
information is processed in your brain that does not enter your consciousness but may still
be stored in the brain: this is called latent learning.

The best study techniques
Extensive research has been on which study techniques are most effective. As it turns
out, the most commonly used techniques do not offer the most return on your learning
time. Rereading, summarizing and highlighting can be useful, but they don't enhance your
understanding as much as thinking more organically about the topics you learned about.
They focus too much on acquiring new information rather than remodeling your brain's
organization of this knowledge. See the following table for an overview of study
techniques and how they fare in scientific research.


--- Page 6 ---
6

Source

It's clear that the best way to enhance your understanding of a topic is deliberate practice.
To that end, we have added 2 sections at the end of every module that we encourage you
to make use of.
1. Learning objectives: you should go through this list and evaluate for each point if
you're capable of doing this.
2. Example exam questions: try to answer these. No answers are provided on
purpose so that you can't cheat and you're forced to formulate questions in the
Facebook group or live Q&A sessions to discuss the materials. Think back on when
you learned math in high school: you can see the answer to a question and think
'ok simple' yet when you then have to get to the answer yourself on your own, you
realize you haven't fully understood it yet.


--- Page 7 ---
7
At the end of the nutrition modules and at the end of the training modules we also have
case study weeks in the course. Here we'll provide you with real-life and theoretical case
studies on how to implement everything you learned thus far in practice. You'll find that
there are some intentional delays in the course materials before all the pieces fall together
to foster deliberate practice, repetition and reflection of the materials and thereby improve
your learning.

We also encourage you to create your own practice materials. The following techniques
are particularly effective based on the research.
• Creating flashcards with questions and terms to explain with the answers on the
back. If you prefer to use your smartphone, you can use the app Anki to create
digital flashcards.
• Print the course materials and black out key terms. Later, go through the document
and fill in the blanks.
• Implement the Cornell Note Taking System for each course module.

How to distribute your studying time
Many students wait until the last minute to study before their exams and then try to cram
in all the information. Psychologists call this 'massing'. As your teachers probably
preached to you on many occasions, massing is not an ideal learning experience. It
certainly beats not learning, but a plethora of research shows spacing your studying time
more evenly over time improves information retention, recall, and general task


--- Page 8 ---
8
performance compared to massing [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]. This is called the 'spacing effect'.
Your memory thrives on repetition, especially for long-term retention. To make use of the
spacing effect, try to plan your learning schedule as follows if possible:
● Plan in one full evening or weekend day to study each week's course materials.
● Plan in 2 shorter sessions later in the week: one to reflect on the learning
objectives and another moment to answer the example exam questions. Based on
your performance, revisit the parts of the module you didn't fully understand yet.

Many students also fall for the planning fallacy: they systematically underestimate how
much time it will take to learn something.

As a result of the above, people learn better when they have set deadlines spaced evenly
apart. Self-imposed deadlines are also effective but not as effective as external deadlines,
presumably because they are not as strict. In line with this, we have spaced the course
contents at a very deliberate pace and we will close off the Comments section for each
module when we start discussing the next module. If you want to ask questions and
engage in the discussion, you'll thus have to keep up with the pace of the course. This set
course structure should also help reduce procrastination.

As a final tip, be strict with yourself. Free up a liberal amount of study time each week in
your agenda, such as Saturday morning, and stick to it. Making up excuses to rationalize
missed deadlines is one of the most common reasons people fail online courses.


--- Page 9 ---
9


--- Page 10 ---
10
Logical reasoning skills
Any intellectual endeavor relies on logical reasoning skills. In this section, we'll give you
many tips to improve your brain gains.

1. Beware of science mumbo-jumbo
'Big words' and technical jargon can give the impression of validity. Don't fall for theories
and plausibility: look for probability and empirical evidence of each theory. Broscience
often relies on theory without evidence.

So always ask yourself if you see any theory: what's the actual evidence supporting this?
Is there direct evidence from randomized controlled experiments on this?


--- Page 11 ---
11
2. Scientific skepticism vs. Bayesian thinking
Scientific consensus is a turtle moving along the sands of truth… Or whatever: it's a slow
process. Scientists only speak of something as a consensus or a well-established finding
if multiple research groups using different methods in different populations have replicated
the finding. That's why it took decades before the scientific community reached an
agreement that androgenic anabolic steroids enhance performance, that strength athletes
benefit from more protein than sedentary individuals and that multiple sets are superior to
single sets for strength development.

The issue here is that science is about knowledge and truth, not about the practical
application thereof. So the scientific perspective on the vast majority of the questions we
find relevant is 'Well, there's research to suggest X, but we face limitations A-Z, so we
need additional research before we can answer this question.' That's an answer that
doesn't benefit anyone and as a coach, it's a cop-out. If you have clients, you have to
advise them something. To get from facts to recommendations, you have to think like a
Bayesian, a rational style of thinking popular in economics that is inspired by the
statistician Thomas Bayes.

A Bayesian estimates a probability of truth for every belief and updates this probability
based on the available evidence. That probability is then multiplied by the expected pay-
offs to come to conclusions (economists call this expected utility theory).

In other words, Bayesian thinking prescribes how scientific evidence - statements of fact
- translate into advice and decisions. When you know someone's goals and the relevant
facts, Bayesian advice optimizes that person's decisions to reach those goals.


--- Page 12 ---
12

Example: You're a sponsored athlete and receive a new test booster. Other than a lot of
research establishing the supplement's safety, there are only 2 studies on its
effectiveness: 1 in favor and one against. A scientific perspective can only answer if it
works, not if you should take it. The answer to 'if it works' in scientific terms will be "No,
more research is needed before we can claim if it works." Yet a Bayesian may think as
follows. The probability that the supplement works is substantial, even though the benefit
is small. The cost of taking the supplement is virtually nil. So the benefit multiplied by the
probability that it works exceeds the cost of taking it. A Bayesian would probably take the
supplement; it's a virtually zero-cost, low-benefit decision.


--- Page 13 ---
13
3. There is no proof, only probability
Proof is the highest standard of evidence. It is normally reserved for logical statements,
such as mathematics. Empirical sciences - the sciences of observable facts - cannot
provide more evidence than probability by definition. Science does not prove supplement
X works; it says it is probable (95% probable, to be specific). When there is no empirical
evidence for a statement, as is often the case for broscientific statements, it can merely be
plausible.

The hierarchy in standards of proof can conveniently be remembered by the 3 Ps. Any
theory can sound plausible, but only with actual data can we say it's probable and we
have to accept that most things will never be proven.


--- Page 14 ---
14
4. It's only a single study!
Imagine there's a new supplement on the market called STX. You want to know if it's
worth buying, so you Google it. You find a forum where someone has tried it and says it
greatly increased his muscle growth. 5 others noticed the same thing.

A blogger did DXA scans to confirm the muscle growth he experienced and it was legit.
He then tested the supplement in a lab and the results match the label's ingredients.

After some more research, you find 3 more people who have confirmed their muscle
growth using MRI after taking STX.

Sounds pretty convincing, right?

The above evidence doesn't come anywhere remotely close to that of a single published
scientific experiment. No subject data, unknown motives and sponsorship, heterogeneous
measurement techniques, no control group, no randomization, no peer-review, no double
blinding, etc.

Don't underestimate the evidence provided by a study. Don't overestimate the worth of an
anecdote.


--- Page 15 ---
15
5. Know the difference between correlation and causation

Source


--- Page 16 ---
16
6. Be skeptical of information from mainstream media


--- Page 17 ---
17
7. Objectivity vs. subjectivity
A statement is either objective or subjective. Objective statements are statements of fact;
they refer to a state of nature. Subjective statements are statements of feeling.

Determining whether a statement is objective or subjective is in itself an objective
question. "Ronnie Coleman is 9 feet tall" is actually a statement of fact, not an opinion. It is
a claim of the state of nature. A false one, obviously, but the statement is still objective.

More common examples are people trying to humbly add 'in my opinion' to a statement of
fact. Similarly, you'll often see people add "and that's a fact" or something along those
lines to strengthen their position. Doing so is at best redundant and often blatantly false.

And this isn't simply a semantic or moralistic distinction. It is crucial. Recall that Bayesian
thinking translates facts into decisions based on someone's goals. Any subjective
statement is entirely meaningless from a Bayesian or scientific perspective. This makes a
great difference in terms of how you give advice, because everything revolves around
someone's goals.

Many questions are subjective or worse, hypothetical (not referring to any facts), and thus
do not have a Bayesian answer. Example: "If you could only do one exercise, which would
it be?" You will be asked these questions, so you will have to help the person asking the
question to reformulate the question in objective terms. In this case, the person is
probably interested in optimizing his or her exercise selection given a time constraint. So
you will have to dig into the person's true intentions and not just take the question for
granted.


--- Page 18 ---
18

Without knowing someone's goals, which is often the case on social media and on
internet forums, the best a Bayesian can often do is provide a statement of fact. For
example, "supplementing BCAAs on top of sufficient dietary protein intake will not
increase strength development, muscle hypertrophy or fat loss." When you know that your
client's goal is one of the above and the client's dietary protein intake is already sufficient,
you can then advise them not to take BCAAs.

Here are some concrete, down-to-earth tips to give more Bayesian advice:
• Be mindful of the person's goals. It sounds so simple, but many coaches give advice
based on their own goals instead of those of their client, like powerlifters advising
clients interested in looking better to get fat to increase squat strength.
• Weigh the costs and benefits of the decision. Things aren't black and white.
• Use contingencies when the person's goals are not known to you. For instance, "If
money is not a concern, these supplements may be worth trying, as they provide a
small performance boost in some of the research."


--- Page 19 ---
19
8. Accept that your ego is weak
When you come across new evidence, you are strongly inclined to find an explanation that
fits with your prior beliefs. This robust finding in psychology is called confirmation bias.
The mind always tries to minimize cognitive dissonance between your ideas. This is
generally a good thing, because a Bayesian should not believe statements that contradict
each other. Having consistent beliefs is fundamental to being rational.

However, accepting that prior beliefs were wrong requires that your self-image takes a hit.
Another fundamental finding in psychology is that humans will go to great lengths to
maintain a positive self-image, since a positive self-image and happiness are closely
related. As such, the mind often finds it easier to 'explain away' new findings than to
accept that old beliefs were wrong.

What can we do? Realize that your mind is imperfect. You are psychologically biased
towards rejecting revolutionary information. Being a Bayesian requires fighting this urge
and overcoming your feelings with reason.


--- Page 20 ---
20
Finding and filtering information
Our goal with this course is to provide you with all the information that's relevant for a
Personal Trainer or recreational strength trainee. However, we encourage you to do your
own research as well, especially after the course. In this section, we'll give you many tips
to turn you into Sherlock Science.

1. Always reading the full text is inefficient
PubMed contains over 27 million articles. About half a million more are published each
year. Even if you read 5,000 articles per year, which means reading 13-14 articles per
day, you're only covering 1% of the new literature.

'Fortunately,' exercise science and nutrition are very small scientific disciplines. It is
possible, if you're efficient, to consume the vast majority of the available information. Still,
hundreds of papers are published each month. The need for efficiency is particularly high
because the quality of these papers is very low compared to more popular scientific
disciplines.

Roughly a hundred interesting articles appear each month. 'Interesting' in this case means
the title suggests the article is relevant for a strength trainee. Then you should generally
read the abstract to determine if it's interesting and only if that piques your interest, should
you bother to read more.


--- Page 21 ---
21
2. Filter your information
In this digital age, lack of information is rarely ever the problem. The problem is knowing
which information to trust. Person A says X, person B says Y. The following guideline
should help you cut through the noise very effectively: if someone doesn't normally
reference his or her work, you can generally ignore whatever facts they claim. Better yet,
don't read their stuff at all. You'll either learn nothing new or you'll gain a question, never
an answer.

Even worse, it's quite likely you'll remember a statement or argument without
remembering its source and forget that it's incorrect. Psychologists call this source
amnesia and it's a major reason for the unreliability of eyewitness testimony.

That said, you may obviously still benefit from someone's experience, perspective or
logical arguments. There just aren't many people who stick to those sorts of claims
without invoking pseudoscience or unsupported facts.

Filtering your information sources will greatly minimize the paralysis by analysis that's so
common in the fitness industry.

Here are several evidence-based fitness authors that Menno vouches for after having
personally met them and worked with them. Note that they don't always agree on
everything and their views may be biased by most of them being friends.
• Brad Schoenfeld
• Bret Contreras


--- Page 22 ---
22
• Borge Fagerli
• Alan Aragon
• Eric Helms
• Greg Nuckols


--- Page 23 ---
23

Interpreting scientific research
Scientific research provides the highest quality of evidence on most topics, but it's not
always easy to interpret scientific publications. In this section, we'll teach you how to read
and interpret scientific publications.

How to read a paper
The most common paper structure in academia is the IMRaD model, illustrated below.


--- Page 36 ---
36

You'll often find a Conclusions section at the end of the Discussion with a summary of the
main findings and their implications.

Way at the end you often find the Acknowledgements section, which is what you should
check for the study funding sources to see if the authors have any conflicts of interest or
potential sponsorship biases.

While this is a useful structure to write up a paper, it is not the order you should read a
paper in if you want to critically evaluate a study. Many study findings make people go
'well, duh.' Yet when you ask people beforehand what they predict the study's outcome is,
they're often wrong. Here's how to read a paper you're genuinely interested in:
1. Read the methods section. This explains what they did.
2. Try to predict what the results will be.
3. Read the results section. This lists the results without any interpretation from the
authors.
4. If your prediction was inaccurate, think about why this was the case.
5. Read the introduction, which should discuss prior literature on the topic, and then
the conclusion, which has the interpretation of the study's findings from the authors.

If you read the introduction first, your view will always be influenced by the perspective of
the researchers. In spite of what you may have learned in Research Methodology 101, the
authors often write the introduction after having seen their own results, so it's a biased
account of the prior literature.


--- Page 37 ---
37

The different types of scientific publications
There are several types of publications. When we think of a 'study', we're often thinking of
a randomized controlled trial (RCT). These generally considered the gold-standard
method to obtain new information about a topic. By randomizing the participants to the
study groups and controlling for all variables, they provide more robust evidence than
most other forms of evidence. If a study did not randomize the participants to the study
groups, the design is sometimes called quasi-experimental.

The lowest level of evidence is a case study or report. This just describes what happened
without any control or intervention, often in a single individual. It's still better than an
anecdote, as it's validated and much information is provided.


--- Page 38 ---
38
A case-control study is one step better, as it compares a group of individuals with a
certain common denominator, like a disease or outstanding academic achievements, with
a control group of individuals that don't have it.

Another level of evidence quality up we have cohort studies. Cohort studies are
essentially case-control studies over a long period. Ideally, cohort studies have a
prospective design, which means the methods were determined beforehand and the
researchers followed the groups of people in real-time. For example, a prospective cohort
study may compare students with low and high academic achievements and follow them
over their life to see who ends up happier.

These separate studies are compiled together in scientific reviews. Some of these are
'narrative' reviews, which means they have no pre-set structure. 'Systematic' reviews are
more controlled in that you have to specify the exact study inclusion criteria and show
which studies you found and excluded.


--- Page 39 ---
39
A systematic review can also be meta-analyzed, which is a method
to take the available information from these studies, notably the
effect sizes, and use statistical calculations to sort of 'average them
out'. For example, if 5 studies show a supplement works and 5 show
it doesn't, we can do a meta-analysis to serve as the arbiter to see if
the overall effect size is positive. It's important to note a meta-
analysis basically recycles information, so if you meta-analyze 5
crappy studies, you often still have poor quality of evidence.
'Garbage in, garbage out.' In this sense, it's better to think of
reviews and meta-analyses as separate lenses looking at the
literature, not as strictly higher-quality evidence. A single well-
designed RCT can provide stronger evidence than a meta-analysis
of 10 low-quality studies.

The above types of publications can have various structures. Here are several research
design structures you should be familiar with.

Cross-over design: This is a type of within-subject design where each participant
undergoes both treatments, ideally in randomized order with a wash-out period in
between. By having each person by his or her own control, you eliminate the variance
from having different people be in the different study groups. Example: Every subject tries
both a high-volume and a low-volume training program to see which works better.

research that compares multiple populations
at a specific point in time. Example:

and looking at their health.

research spanned over a certain period with
repeated measurements.

Observational research: This means the study did not have an intervention. It merely
measured what happened in a certain situation.

Epidemiology: This is a collective term for unrandomized, observational research,

self-reported data. It includes mainly cross-sectional and longitudinal research with case-

Scientific publications are filled with technical terms lay populations may not understand.
Here's a mini-dictionary to understand the research jargon.

research, drop-out rates are typically high, as some participants can't adhere to the

--- Page 44 ---

External validity: The degree to which the results of the study can be generalized beyond
the study sample, to other situations, times and people. Do the same results occur in
other settings?

Internal validity: The degree to which the study's methods allow us to answer its
research question. In other words, whether we can establish causality between the
independent variable that was manipulated and the dependent variable that was
observed. If a study sets out to determine if carbohydrate intake influences performance,
but it does not control for energy and protein intake, it has low internal validity, as the
results are confounded by energy and protein intake. We cannot know which of those 3
factors influenced the results.

Interaction effects: An effect of a combination of two or more independent variables on a
dependent variable. For example, ephedrine and caffeine have an interaction effect: their
combined effect is greater than the combined effects of both in isolation. They work
synergistically, not just additively.
Outliers: An observation in the data set that is much different than the other observations in the data

Peer-review: The scrutiny of a paper before its
publication by other researchers. Ideally, this is done anonymously from both sides to
avoid bias, mediated by the editor of the publishing journal.
