Statistics 101
Science relies on the collection of data. To interpret those data, we need to perform
statistical analyses. As such, if you don't understand statistical methods, you can't truly
understand any research findings. Unfortunately, most current scientific fields rely strongly
on frequentist statistics. These statistical methods are often very counterintuitive (unlike
say Bayesian statistics).

In the following lecture, we'll provide a simplified interpretation of common statistical terms
and analyses. Below we go into more detail and formal definitions.

➢ Lecture
Stats 101

Statistical significance
In exercise and nutritional sciences, you often want to know whether a certain treatment
worked better than placebo. Say training program A results in 2% growth and program B
results in 3% growth. Is A more effective than B? To determine this, we can test for
statistical significance with a probability (p) value. If the p-value is above 0.05, we say the
difference is not statistically significant. 0.05 is an arbitrary but firmly engrained
convention.


--- Page 49 ---
49
The p-value measures the probability of obtaining all results in the study under the
assumption that all the statistical model's assumptions are correct, including the null
hypothesis. The null hypothesis (H0) is the theory that there is no difference between the
study groups. As such, a low p-value means the chance of observing the results we see is
low if we assume there is in reality no difference between the groups. The results are too
extreme to be due to chance. As such, you can roughly think of the p-value as being the
probability that the results are due to chance. That actual probability is higher, 23% based
on some estimates, but the intuition is the same: a low p-value supports that there is in
fact a difference between groups.

Why can't we just say 3% is greater than 2% and leave it at that? There are virtually
always confounding factors that induce variance in any study. For example, some people
train harder than others. If you happen to have more hardcore people in one group than
the other, that may be the reason they grew more from a certain strength training protocol
than others, even though the protocols were equally effective physiologically speaking. If
you plot the muscle growth rates of both groups, you'll get 2 distributions. If there is no
overlap between these distributions at all, it's easy to see there is a difference in muscle
growth between these groups: the worst gains in one group were still better than the best
gains in the other group. However, in practice the graphs will overlap and a statistical
significance test then tells us if these 2 distributions are likely part of the same distribution
or if they're in fact different distributions.


--- Page 50 ---
50

Example: You plot the increase in total training tonnage of program 1 with sample 1 and
program 2 with sample 2. In this case, while the average increase in tonnage of program 2
is higher, this difference is so small relative to the variance in individual results that we
cannot say the distributions of the results are really different from each other. It's likely
sample 1 and sample 2 achieved the same distribution of results (plotted in orange).

In short, statistical significance means there is most likely a true difference between
groups, not just between-subject variance or measurement error. Assuming the study was
well-designed (high internal validity), we can conclude this effect was caused by the


--- Page 51 ---
51
independent variable of the study and, for example, program A was more effective than
program B.

Statistical significance vs. effect size
'Statistically significant' intuitively sounds like it means something is highly relevant, like
the effect is big. However, statistical significance does not directly measure the magnitude
of an effect. Large effects are more likely to be statistically significant, but very small
effects that are practically insignificant can also reach statistical significance. To look at
the strength of an effect, we can look at the raw values or the effect size, which is a
standardized measure of the strength of an effect. Effect sizes can be computed in
various ways, but most rely on dividing the average difference by the standard deviation.

The formulas for various effect sizes.

As an example to contrast effect size with statistical significance, in a very large study of
supplement X, we may find that testosterone increases by 3% in the experimental group
and this is significantly more than the placebo group. Testosterone production fluctuates


--- Page 52 ---
52
by about 50% across the day in healthy men, so 3% is a trivial increase and the effect size
is small.

As an opposite example, let's say a group of researchers try to replicate the above finding,
but their measurement instruments are older than Gandalf, so the measurements are all
over the place. They find a 178% increase in testosterone, but individual readings vary
from -340 to +421% and the finding does not reach statistical significance. In this
scenario, we cannot even talk about the effect size, since we cannot say with any
certainty that this finding is not just measurement error. However, depending on the
theoretical basis and the other literature, these kinds of findings can indicate a trend. So
just because statistical significance was not reached, it does not mean that this finding is
uninteresting. This brings us to the discussion of statistical power.

Statistical power
The power of a statistical test is the probability that the test will reject the null hypothesis
when the null hypothesis is false. It's identical to the probability of not making a type II
error (failing to reject a false null hypothesis): 1 minus power = type II error probability. For
those unfamiliar with the statistical jargon, here's a more intuitive explanation: statistical
power is the ability to detect an effect with a study when there is a truly an effect. It is the
sensitivity of a test: sensitivity in this case is both an intuitive and the proper statistical
terminology.

Here's an example in lay terms. You're conducting a study on the efficacy of supplement
X. There's the physical reality of its effectiveness, unknown to you, and there's the


--- Page 53 ---
53
outcome of your statistical test. Let's suppose that supplement X is the holy grail of
muscle building supps and transforms you into the incredible hulk in a manner of weeks. If
that's also what your test shows, everyone's happy. However, if supplement X actually
works, but your test says it doesn't, you've failed to detect an effect even though there
actually was one. That's a type II error and it's caused by a lack of statistical power. A
more sensitive test would have found an effect. (Lack of) statistical power is an important
reason why different studies can show different things.

What determines the statistical power of a test? A full power analysis takes into account
many things, including the design of the experiment, but there are 3 main factors.
1. The statistical significance criterion we call alpha. You can basically forget about
this, because the convention of using a significance level of 0.05 has become so
deeply ingrained in the scientific literature it may as well have been enforced by
law.

2. Sample size: The larger the sample size of a study, the easier it is to detect effects.
In our example, if the group receiving supplement X and the placebo group both
consisted of thousands of people, it's most likely that any difference between the
groups at the end of the study was caused by supplement X, because the large
sample size cancels out between-subject variance and ensures that the observed
values are very close to the actual population means (provided the sampling was
random and the study was well controlled). A sample size of only a few participants
has far less statistical power, because the effect of supplement X is obscured by
random variation or noise. In such a small sample, just one individual with, say, a
heart condition that makes him or her unsusceptible to the effects of supplement X


--- Page 54 ---
54
may be enough to fool the test into thinking supplement X doesn't work.

3. Third, there's the effect size, which is, as the name suggests, the magnitude of the
effect you're looking for. In our example, the effect size of supplement X is
extremely high, because it turns you into the incredible bulk. If that were literally the
case, you only really need a sample size of one or two people to know supplement
X is the bomb. Some things can have such a large effect size that personal
experience and anecdotes are enough evidence to see it's true. This was the case
with injectable testosterone when it first came on the market. Researchers debated
for decades whether injecting testosterone resulted in increased muscle growth,
mostly using absurdly small dosages, while it was blatantly obvious to bodybuilders
that had injected it that it increased their gains. In contrast, if supplement X were
only mildly effective, you'd need a far larger sample to detect its effects.

Type II errors can be contrasted with type I errors, which are false positives. A small
sample increases the chance of not just type II but also type I errors. If you happen to
have 3 genetic freaks in a study of 5 people, you may observe a statistically significant
difference between groups with a large effect size that has nothing to do with the study's
independent variable. It's just that those 3 people grew so much more muscle than the
others regardless of which group they were in because of their genetics.


--- Page 55 ---
55

Good studies perform an a priori power analysis, which means they estimate the power of
their study based on effect sizes from previous data or theory. An a priori power
estimation can be used to determine how many subjects are needed to reach 80% power,
which is commonly regarded as the minimum needed for a viable study.

Importantly, statistical power should not be calculated with the study's own observed
effect size after the study has completed (post-hoc). Using the study's own effect size
makes it useless, as it just becomes a recalculation of the p-value. If you question the
observed p-value, it's better to look at the confidence interval of the effect than post-hoc
power.

Confidence intervals
Scientific publications commonly report 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Confidence
intervals are interval estimates, as opposed to a fixed-point estimate. For example, if you
want to know how long the average dog is, you can measure 91 dogs and your average
may be 69 cm. That's probably not exactly the true average length in the whole population
of dogs. A confidence interval illustrates this by computing a range of values rather than


--- Page 56 ---
56
just reporting the average. The 95% CI is typically calculated as being 2 standard
deviations below the average up to 2 standard deviations above the average, as
illustrated below. In a normal distribution, we can say that 95% of our sample values fall
within this range. You can also compute the confidence interval for an effect, like that of
the difference in muscle growth rate between 2 groups.

Importantly, a confidence interval says 95% of the sample values fall within its range
under valid model assumptions. This does not mean 95% of the true (population) values
fall within this range. In theory, if one would repeat the study a near-infinite number of
times, 95% of confidence intervals should contain the true value (assuming all analysis
assumptions were correct in the first place), but a single confidence interval may not
contain the true value. As such, a 95% confidence interval does not mean there is a 95%
chance the true value falls within this range. There is such a statistic in Bayesian
statistics: it's called a credible interval.


--- Page 57 ---
57

However, we can still roughly interpret a 95% CI as a range of potential values that are
more compatible with the study's data than values outside of this range. It's particularly
useful to know if the CI spans the null. If the CI of an effect size, such as the increase in
muscle growth from a supplement, does not include zero, this is strong evidence for the
effectiveness of the supplement. If the effect size CI does include zero, we cannot exclude
the supplement doesn't work.
Noise vs. bias
Many people overlook this distinction. Plenty of study reviews try to assess the truth of a
study's findings by listing its strengths and weaknesses instead of, as a Bayesian,
inferring probability from statistical principles. You can't just list the limitations of a study
and then conclude 'therefore: screw this study.'

When you want to discredit a finding, you need to demonstrate bias, not just noise.

Example: A study finds that a greater meal frequency results in higher nitrogen balance in
trained but not untrained individuals. If the trained group had a higher protein intake than
the untrained group, that would be bias: the higher nitrogen balance in the trained group
could have been the result of this increased protein intake and not the meal frequency.

That's bias: a systematic flaw in the internal validity of the study that predisposes the
study's results in a specific direction.


--- Page 58 ---
58
Stating that nitrogen balance is an unreliable measurement technique is an example of
noise. Noise reduces statistical power. If you understood statistical power, you should now
understand that statistical significance in spite of this noise is actually an argument in
favor of the study's relevance. This suggests the effect size or the reliability of the effect
was high.

Most study limitations and weaknesses people use to discredit a study finding they don't
want to believe are noise, not bias. Common biases in exercise and nutritional literature
are:
• Not controlling for a treatment imbalance in calories, protein or physical work
between groups.
• Having a sample imbalance by having one group that is fatter or less experienced
than the other group.

Common sources of noise include:
• Small sample sizes.
• Large sample heterogeneity (variation) due to having people with varying training
experience in the experiment and the inherent genetic variability in responsiveness
to exercise.
• Measurement error: the best measurement instruments are expensive and exercise
science is a poorly funded field.
